rule.desc: Use generic types for reusable components that work with multiple types. Prefer Python 3.12+ native syntax with automatic variance inference.
rule.rationale: Generics enable type-safe reuse without duplication. They preserve type information through transformations. Python 3.12+ syntax is cleaner and automatically infers variance from usage, eliminating manual TypeVar declarations.

example.code:
# Good - generic function def first[T](items: list[T]) -> T | None: """Get first item, preserving type.""" return items[0] if items else None
# Usage - types are checked data_tool: DataTool[list[Transaction]] = DataTool( parser=CsvParser(), producer=TransactionProducer() )
# Legacy - TypeVar + Generic (pre-3.12) from typing import TypeVar, Generic
T = TypeVar("T")
class DataTool(Generic[T]): """Old syntax - requires separate TypeVar and Generic.""" def __init__(self, parser: Parser[T], producer: Producer[T]): self.parser = parser self.producer = producer

rule.tool_support: Python 3.12+ required. mypy 1.7+ supports this syntax. For older Python versions, use TypeVar + Generic.

notes.note: - **Cleaner code**: No separate TypeVar declarations - **Automatic variance**: Python infers covariance/contravariance from usage - **Better readability**: Generic parameters visible in class/function signature - **Fewer imports**: No need to import TypeVar, Generic
